items: When a new queen takes over in lineage mode, your hive is no longer wiped: built comb and cells stay, and the colony’s timeline keeps going.
About half your workers stay with the new queen.
Honey you spend rerolling or upgrading in the succession shop is removed from nectar cells when you confirm, so the budget matches what leaves your stores.

The launch menu now offers Casual, Normal, and Custom ways to start a colony: Casual skips intruders and the lineage system; Normal keeps them on; Custom lets you set days per season, how many workers you start with, and those toggles before play.
Your choices are stored with that save; continue slots can show a crown when lineage is on and a marker when intruders are on.
Older colonies you already had keep the lineage system; only new games use the new defaults.
A Build bar at the bottom of the screen lets you pick brood, pollen, or nectar cell types.
Choosing brood, pollen, or nectar for a cell opens a compact menu above that cell with matching hive art, instead of a full-screen dimmed dialog.
Tap an icon to confirm, or lift your finger on an icon after touching the cell; tap away on the map, pan the hive, or press Escape to close without picking.
The queen’s reign now ends with mandatory succession after three full years (60-day seasons each), instead of five.
Built cells that still need a cell type get a soft golden outline so unfinished spots are easier to notice.
You can keep several colony saves in this browser: pick one to continue, delete saves you do not need, or start a new game for another slot (new colonies appear in the list after the first autosave).
Bees moving between hive levels pause briefly at the junction with wing-flap animation and a subtle size pulse before they step onto the next comb.
Unhappy bee thought bubbles now show the ‼️ reliably; they could look empty before.
Unhappy bees (hungry or thirsty) show a small ‼️ thought bubble on the hive so you can spot who needs care.
Hungry workers search every hive level for the nearest food and walk there—even if the pollen or nectar cell is on another comb.
Unprocessed nectar in the HUD is only what’s stored in nectar cells—workers eat from those cells or honey, not a separate colony nectar pool.
Brood: eggs hatch a little faster; larvae need more pollen deliveries but fewer nectar ones; the queen attempts to lay more often.
Hunger and thirst rise faster, but the “happy” band is wider; foraging trips are shorter and each return brings less pollen and nectar.
Feeding an adult from a nectar cell uses less nectar per meal.
Hive cells use new illustrated sprite art instead of flat colored circles.
Brood, pollen, and nectar or honey cells change appearance as they fill or mature; foundations fade in while you build.
Starting a new colony (not Continue) launches an interactive tutorial: it walks you through building, brood and food cells, panning the view, and changing levels.
You can skip the tutorial anytime; finishing or skipping is remembered in this browser.
A loading screen appears while the game prepares assets; the menu fades in when ready.
Background music starts after you choose New game or Continue.
The launch screen can show a short list of gameplay changes after an update.
Use Got it to dismiss; your browser remembers the last version you saw.
